hook: You already know how squaring a number works — but what happens when you need to go backwards, finding a number whose square gives you a target value? This unit unlocks that reverse operation, the radical, and shows you it's hiding everywhere from GPS distances to architectural blueprints. Get ready to discover how a simple square root connects algebra, geometry, and the real world.
concept: The unifying idea is the relationship between powers and their inverses — just as subtraction undoes addition, radicals undo exponents, and understanding this lets you solve equations, graph new functions, and measure the world around you. This is formalized through the concept of inverse operations applied to exponential expressions.

topic_roadmap.description: You will learn to evaluate $\sqrt{x}$ and $\sqrt[3]{x}$ by recognizing perfect squares and perfect cubes, building the foundation for everything that follows in this unit.
Master rewriting radical expressions in simplest form by factoring out perfect squares or cubes, so that you can work with them cleanly in equations and functions.

topic_roadmap.topic_name: Graphing Square Root and Cube Root Functions
topic_roadmap.description: Apply transformations to sketch the graphs of $f(x) = \sqrt{x}$ and $f(x) = \sqrt[3]{x}$, identifying their domain, range, and how shifts and stretches change their shape.
You will learn to isolate and eliminate radicals by raising both sides of an equation to a power, then check for extraneous solutions that can sneak in during the process.
Apply the relationship $a^2 + b^2 = c^2$ to find missing side lengths in right triangles, putting your radical skills to work in a classic geometric context.
You will use the Distance Formula — a direct application of the Pythagorean Theorem — alongside the Midpoint Formula to precisely measure and locate points on the coordinate plane.
